Identifying the author's argument structure (claims, evidence, and reasoning) within the text
Evaluating how the author integrates and responds to opposing viewpoints
Distinguishing the author's position from rival positions the author represents or attributes to others
Foundations of comprehension: building basic understanding of a text before reasoning within it
Reasoning beyond the text uses outside ideas; within-text reasoning must exclude them, so the boundary must be understood
Comprehension of the passage's main idea, purpose, and central thesis
Determining authorial purpose, tone, and rhetorical intent
Identifying argument components and distinguishing claim from supporting evidence
Drawing inferences that are directly implied by the passage
Integrating distinct passage components into a coherent whole
